summary:
The Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (USSR) was formally established on 30 December 1922, when the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic, the Ukrainian SSR, the Byelorussian SSR, and the Transcaucasian SFSR signed the Treaty of Creation. The new state was the institutional successor to the Russian Empire and the Provisional Government, governed by the Bolshevik Party (soon renamed the Communist Party of the Soviet Union) under a single-party dictatorship nominally structured around soviets — elected workers' councils — but in practice controlled by the party apparatus.

The founding of the Soviet Union completed the Bolshevik consolidation of power following the [[event:2646|October Revolution]] and the [[event:2647|civil war]]. Vladimir Lenin's New Economic Policy (NEP), introduced in 1921, partially restored market mechanisms to revive an economy devastated by years of conflict, and the early Soviet state experimented with federalism and cultural autonomy for national minorities. These pragmatic concessions did not, however, soften the fundamental one-party dictatorship or the repressive apparatus of the Cheka secret police.

Following Lenin's death in 1924, a power struggle culminated in Joseph Stalin's consolidation of control by 1928–1929. Stalin dismantled the NEP and imposed forced collectivization of agriculture, which combined with deliberate grain seizures caused the catastrophic [[event:3333|Holodomor]] famine in Ukraine and mass death across the Soviet Union. The [[event:3332|Great Purge]] of 1936–1938 killed hundreds of thousands and sent millions to the Gulag labor camp system. Despite these horrors, rapid forced industrialization through Five-Year Plans made the Soviet Union an industrial power capable of eventually defeating Nazi Germany in [[event:112|World War II]].

The Soviet Union went on to become a superpower locked in competition with the United States during the [[event:113|Cold War]], acquiring nuclear weapons, launching the [[event:2676|Space Race]], and supporting Communist movements worldwide. Its history is inseparable from the entire trajectory of twentieth-century global politics. The [[event:2682|dissolution of the Soviet Union]] in December 1991 ended the experiment after 69 years, leaving fifteen successor states and unresolved questions about the legacy of Communist governance.
